rulings.Case Title: DIEGO ORNELAS VS. NATIONAL STORAGE AFFILIATES TRUST, A MARYLAND REAL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Unopposed Motion & Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Joint Stipulation Of Class Action Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 3, PLAINTIFF DIEGO ORNELAS' Motion For Preliminary Approval Of Joint Stipulation Of Class Action Settlement. The motion is denied without prejudice. The next iteration may, preferably, be (1) a new motion that does not rely on documents filed in connection with this motion, or (2), if only a few modifications are required, consist of supplementary material. A new settlement agreement may be needed. A new proposed Notice will be required. All further motions should be noticed before Judge Karnow in Dept. 611, after contacting his clerk for a hearing date. Paper courtesy copies are required. After preliminary approval, the final hearing will also be before Judge Karnow in Dept. 611. The parties are generally referred to Weil & Brown, et al., California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 14:138.20 (Rutter: 2020), and an article with useful tips, https://works.bepress.com/curtis_karnow/18/. The Court's full tentative ruling is emailed to the parties. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/CEK)

rulings.Case Title: ADAM FUMAROLA VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Adam Fumarola Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendants To Answer Plaintiff'S Request For Form Interrogatories, Set One, Special Interrogatories,Set One, Request For Production, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And/Or Their Attorneys In The Amount Of $2,100. (Cont. Per Agreement Of The Parties. Email Dated 7/14/20)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 3, Plaintiff Adam Fumarola's Motion To Compel Defendants To Answer Plaintiff'S Request For Form Interrogatories, Set One, Special Interrogatories,Set One, Request For Production, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And/Or Their Attorneys In The Amount Of $2,100 (Part 1 of 2 for Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel defendants to answer plaintiff's form interrogatories, set one, special interrogatories, set one, and request for production, set one, is denied as moot except as to sanctions because defendants served code compliant responses to the pending discovery requests before the filing of plaintiff's amended notice of motion and this hearing. Under these circumstances, the court has discretion whether to hear the pending motion. Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 390, 408-409. Sinaiko teaches that one consideration in the exercise of such discretion is whether the interrogatory responses were in bad faith and intended "merely [to] obstruct and delay discovery." Laguna Auto Body v. Farmers Ins. Exchange, 231 Cal.App.3d 481, 487 (1991). The court finds no such bad faith or improper intention to be present here. In plaintiff's reply in support of the motion, plaintiff argues that the verifications to the discovery responses are insufficient because the verifications were supposedly signed "on information and belief" and not under oath. To begin with, this argument is raised for the first time in plaintiff's reply and cannot be considered now as a matter of fairness. Reichardt v. Hoffman, 52 Cal.App.4th 754, 764 (1997); American Drug Stores, Inc. v. Stroh, 10 Cal. App.4th 1446, 1453 (1992); Varjabedian v. City of Madera, 20 Cal.3d 285, 295, fn. 11 (1997). In any case, plaintiff's new argument is incorrect. The Verifications explain that the information in each response came from "numerous sources" within the corporate defendants and that the signing representative is authorized to act for the corporate defendant and is therefore its agent. Finally, the signing representative acknowledges a lack of personal knowledge as to each specific response but "believe[s] and [is] informed" that the matters stated are true and correct. Then, the agent of the corporate defendant declares under penalty of perjury that the contents of the verification are true and correct. Nothing more was required. (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ADAM FUMAROLA VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Adam Fumarola'S Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Deeming Admitted Truth Of Facts And Genuineness Of Documents And Imposing Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And/Or Their Attorneys Of Record In The Amount Of $1,800 (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 3, Plaintiff Adam Fumarola's Motion For Order Deeming Admitted Truth Of Facts And Genuineness Of Documents And Imposing Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And/Or Their Attorneys Of Record In The Amount Of $1,800 (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling) In addition, the first claimed deficiency, as to requests nos. 1 and 2 is essentially a dispute as to the veracity of the response, but that kind of claim cannot be resolved in a law and motion hearing. The remaining claims are disputed in defendants' Reply to Opposition to their Motion for Relief from Waiver of Objections at page 5. These disputed claims of insufficient responses do not change the court's conclusion that defendants' responses substantially complied with Code of Civil Procedure Section 2033.220. Accordingly, the motion is denied as moot and under the terms of Code of Civil Procedure Section 2033.280(c). Under the circumstances, the sanctions would be unjust and will be denied. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jameshfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. (End of Tentative Ruling) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ADAM FUMAROLA VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Adam Fumarola Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Deeming Admitted Truth Of Facts And Genuineness Of Documents And Imposing Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And/Or Their Attorneys Of Record In The Amount Of $1,800.(Cont. Per Agreement Of The Parties. Email Dated 7/14/20)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 3, Plaintiff Adam Fumarola's Motion For Order Deeming Admitted Truth Of Facts And Genuineness Of Documents And Imposing Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And/Or Their Attorneys Of Record In The Amount Of $1,800 (Part 1 of 2 for Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to for order deeming admitted the truth of facts and genuineness of documents is denied as moot except as to sanctions because defendants served code compliant responses to each Request for Admissions, Set No. One, before the filing of plaintiff's amended notice of motion and this hearing. As explained as to the accompanying motion to compel responses to interrogatories and requests for production, the court has discretion whether to hear the pending motion with one consideration in the exercise of such discretion being whether the responses were in bad faith and with improper intent. As with the companion motion, the court finds no such bad faith or improper intention to be present here. In addition and as stated in the order as to defendants' motion for relief from waiver of objections, court is satisfied that defendants have served proposed responses to requests for admission which substantially complied with Code of Civil Procedure Section 2033.220. Accordingly, the motion to deem matters admitted cannot be granted. Code of Civil Procedure Section 2033.280(c). See Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 390, 408-409. In plaintiff's reply in support of the motion, as in the companion motion as to interrogatories and requests for production, plaintiff argues that the verifications to the responses are insufficient because the verifications were supposedly signed "on information and belief" and not under oath. As explained in the order on the companion motion, this argument was made for the first time in plaintiff's reply and cannot be considered now. In addition, plaintiff is incorrect as to the asserted defects in the verifications. The verifications are entirely sufficient. Plaintiff's reply also asserts for the first time in the reply brief several claimed defects in the responses. Again, matters raised for the first time in a reply brief cannot be considered because of the unfairness thereby imposed on the opposing party. (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ADAM FUMAROLA VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Adam Fumarola'S Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendants To Answer Plaintiff'S Request For Form Interrogatories, Set One, Special Interrogatories,Set One, Request For Production, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And/Or Their Attorneys In The Amount Of $2,100 (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 3, Plaintiff Adam Fumarola's Motion To Compel Defendants To Answer Plaintiff'S Request For Form Interrogatories, Set One, Special Interrogatories,Set One, Request For Production, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And/Or Their Attorneys In The Amount Of $2,100 (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling) Plaintiff's secondary authority is not to the contrary and no reliable authority supports the notion that a corporation is required to retain an omniscient officer or agent to verify discovery responses nor that every knowledgeable contributor to a corporate discovery response must each execute a verification. On the contrary, it is well known that a corporate agent need not have personal knowledge of the information sought in order to answer on behalf of the corporation but may consult with and gather information from other individuals in the corporation. See, e.g., International Ass'n. of Machinists, Dist. 169 v. Amana Refrig., Inc., 90 F.R.D. 1, 2 (E.D. Tenn. 1978). And even if the verifications were somehow technically defective, no authority supports the proposition that a defective verification is entirely void and further voids the entire response. The only judicial authority plaintiff cites, Appleton v. Superior Court, 206 Cal.App.3d 632, 636 (1988), does not so hold. These verifications are entirely sufficient. Plaintiff also contends that some of the responses are insufficient or incomplete in certain respects. These claims are contested in defendants' Reply to Opposition to their Motion for Relief from Waiver of Objections and, in any case, are insufficient to show that the responses were not made in good faith or with improper intent. Accordingly, the court concludes that the motion is moot and denies it on that basis. Under the circumstances, the award of sanctions would be unjust and will be denied. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jameshfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. (End of Tentative Ruling) =(302/JPT)

In addition to the foregoing, the court notes that granting relief from waiver will not impose any prejudice on plaintiff, the trial having been continued to May 24, 2021. Accordingly, based on the showing of mistake, inadvertence or excusable neglect and of prior service of substantially code compliant responses, the motion for relief from waiver of objections is granted. rulings.Case Title: ADAM FUMAROLA VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Rasier, Llc, Rasier-Ca, Llc, Uber Technologies, Inc. Defendants Uber Technologies Inc Rasier Llc And Rasier-Ca Llcs Noitice Of Motion And Motion For Relief For Waiver Of Objections (Cont. Per Agreement Of The Parties. Email Dated 7/14/20.)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 3, Defendants Uber Technologies Inc, Rasier LLC, and Rasier-CA LLC's Motion For Relief For Waiver Of Objections (Part 1 of 2 for Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion for relief from waiver of objections is granted pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure Sections 2030.290(a), 2031.300(a) and 2033.280(a). The court finds that defendants' failure to timely respond stemmed from mistake, inadvertence or excusable neglect. The individual attorney responsible for defendants' representation changed firms at essentially the same time that the discovery requests were propounded. The original defense firm forwarded the requests by email but the email was lost or misplaced such that the responsible attorney did not become aware of the requests until a few days after the due date for responses. Defense counsel immediately explained the circumstances to plaintiff's counsel and requested an extension of time to respond. Plaintiff's counsel refused the extension except on condition no objections be interposed. Thereafter, defense counsel served responses to the discovery requests. This motion followed. The court is also satisfied that defendants' responses substantially complied with Code of Civil Procedure Sections 2030.210, 2030.220, 2030.230 and 2030.240 (applicable to written interrogatories), Sections 2031.210, 2031.220, 2031.230 and 2031.240 (applicable to demands for production) and Sections 2033.210, 2033.220, and 2030.230 (applicable to requests for admissions). Plaintiff contends that some of the responses are insufficient or incomplete in certain respects. These contentions, however, are disputed by defendants and are themselves insufficient to demonstrate that the responses are not in substantial compliance with the foregoing code sections. (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CAROL HASS VS. AIRBNB INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT On Plaintiffs Entire Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 4, DEFENDANT PETER ASPEREN, KIYOMI ASPEREN's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT On Plaintiffs Entire Complaint. Defendants Peter and Kiyomi Van Asperen's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendants fail to maintain their initial burden of production. The pleadings identify the issues to be considered on a motion for summary judgment. Wattenbarger v. Cincinnati Reds, Inc. (1994) 28 Cal.App.4th 746, 750 ["The defendant must present facts to negate each claim as framed by the complaint or to establish a defense. Only then must the plaintiff demonstrate the existence of a triable, material issue of fact"]. The complaint alleges among other things that defendants were negligent by (1) not "warning Plaintiff of the hazardous condition"; (2) not "installing and maintaining artificial light sufficient in volume to illuminate the pathway"; and (3) not "avoiding tours during evening hours when it is dark." Complaint, pgs. 4-5 ( 14). Civil Code section 1714(a) provides that "[e]veryone is responsiblefor an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person." Here, a reasonable trier of fact could conclude that defendants were negligent by conducting a nighttime tour of the premises without sufficient lighting. Defendants' motion does not sufficiently address this theory of liability. The trier of fact will also resolve all issues of comparative fault and whether Plaintiff was negligent by e.g., failing to decline defendant Kiyomi Van Asperen's invitation for a night tour. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/CEK)

rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 5, 2-CROSS COMPLAINANT DPR CONSTRUCTION's Motion For Leave To File First Amended Cross-Complaint. Granted. There is no substantial showing of prejudice and there is time for adequate discovery. While there may be legal issues concerning the causes of action, leave to amend would likely be granted at least in the first instance, and handling the issues also at this stage is a waste of time. The issues may be treated in Redwood's response to the amended cross complaint. The new cross complaint must be filed not later than 26 August, 2020. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/CEK)

rulings.Case Title: RONALD ANTHONY YATES VS. KATHRYN R. DEVINCENZI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Further Responses
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 2, Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Further Responses (Part 1 of 3 For Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling). Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel further responses to demand for inspection and production of documents is granted to the following extent: Defendant shall serve code compliant responses to demands 2, 4, 6, and 7 and produce all responsive documents (to the extent not already produced) on or before September 30, 2020. Defendant's objections except as to privilege are overruled. Counsel are ordered to meet and confer as to the terms of a protective order as to any claimed rights of privacy and shall submit the proposed protective order to the temporary judge at jameshfleming@gmail.com in a single document in Word format within seven days of the date of this order. Any disputes as to the language or terms of the protective order shall be highlighted in the proposed protective order. Sanctions are denied because plaintiffs have acted reasonably and defendant, while failing to comply with the Code of Civil Procedure, has nevertheless produced a significant number of requested documents and appears to have acted in good faith. The award of sanctions would therefore be unjust. The Code of Civil Procedure Sections 2031.210 et seq. specifies with precision the requirements for a response to demand for inspection of documents - to state under oath whether the responding party will comply with the demand; whether the responding party lacks the ability to comply and why so; or an objection. Instead of following these requirements, plaintiff has inserted a condition of reasonableness onto her responses and has gone to extreme lengths to explain what she did to respond. It may be that defendant has produced everything she has, but defendant has never provided code compliant responses so it is impossible to tell. (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: RONALD ANTHONY YATES VS. KATHRYN R. DEVINCENZI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Further Responses (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 2, Plaintiff's Motion To Compel Further Responses (Part 2 Of 3 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling) In addition, defendant claims that, because certificate of deposit account pertaining to the subject property was reported together with her personal accounts, her financial privacy may be jeopardized by these discovery requests. Given that defendant chose the manner of holding the accounts in question, her actions appear to have amounted to a waiver or at least weakened her claim of privacy. Defendant's privacy claim is subject to the familiar balancing test. Puerto v. Superior Court, 158 Cal. App. 4th 1242, 1251 (2008); see Pioneer Electronics (USA), Inc., 40 Cal. 4th at 371 (holding that "privacy interest[s] . . . must be measured against other competing or countervailing interests in a 'balancing test'"); see also Williams v. Superior Court, 3 Cal. 5th 531, 557 (2017) (approving the balancing test and "disapprov[ing]" any rule that "require[s] a party seeking discovery of private information to always establish a compelling interest or compelling need . . . in every case"). In this instance, defendant's privacy interest is outweighed by plaintiffs' interests in preparing their case and "the historically important state interest of facilitating the ascertainment of truth in connection with legal proceedings". In re Lifschutz, 2 Cal.3d 415, 432 (1970). Any undue intrusion into defendant's personal finances will be prevented by the agreed protective order referred to above. Such an order, possibly including redaction and in camera review provisions, would limit any intrusion and amply protect plaintiff's privacy interests. Defendant seeks to rely on the delayed discovery rule and the statute of limitations to preclude discovery beyond that which has been produced. However, defendant overlooks plaintiffs' allegations that the parties jointly owned real property but defendant solely controlled all records and receipts of income for the property (Complaint Para. 19). This describes a partnership or at least a relationship of special trust. A partnership imposes fiduciary duties (Corporations Code Section 16404) and a special trust relationship merits similar treatment as respects delayed discovery. Moreno v. Sanchez, 106 Cal.App.4th 1415, 1424 (2003). Defendant's citation of WA Southwest 2, LLC v. First American Title Ins. Co., 240 Cal. App. 4th 148 (2015), explains the fiduciary exception to the discovery rule which postpones accrual of a cause of action until the plaintiff discovers, or has reason to discover, the cause of action. Fox v. Ethicon Endo-Surgery, Inc., 35 Cal.4th 797, 807 (2005). The fiduciary exception goes further and limits the "reason to discover" duty of inquiry. That is, when a potential plaintiff with a fiduciary individual, that plaintiff's burden of discovery is reduced and he is entitled to rely on the statements provided by the fiduciary. Eisenbaum v. Western Energy Resources, Inc., 218 Cal.App.3d 314, 324 (1990). Plaintiffs argue that such is the case here, where they received only statements prepared by defendant and not the underlying bank records. Notably, defendant does not argue that plaintiffs were put on notice of a breach of fiduciary duty by defendant and thereby incurred a duty of inquiry. Miller v. Bechtel Corp., 33 Cal.3d 868, 874-875 (1983). Under these circumstances it makes no difference if plaintiffs could independently have investigated defendant's accounts because they were not on notice of any problems and the statute was tolled until (if ever) they discover wrongdoing. Accordingly, plaintiffs' discovery is not temporally limited by the discovery rule or the statute of limitations. (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 3 of 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NEOREACH INC. VS. VENTOS LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 10, DEFENDANT VETNOS, INC.'s DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. Defendant's demurrer to the third cause of action for intentional interference with contractual relations is sustained with leave to amend. An amended complaint must be filed not later than August 31, 2020. "The elements which a plaintiff must plead to state the cause of action for intentional interference with contractual relations are (1) a valid contract between plaintiff and a third party; (2) defendant's knowledge of this contract; (3) defendant's intentional acts designed to induce a breach or disruption of the contractual relationship; (4) actual breach or disruption of the contractual relationship; and (5) resulting damage." Pacific Gas & Electric Co. v. Bear Stearns & Co. (1990) 50 Cal.3d 1118, 1126 (internal citations omitted). Plaintiff's allegations support the claim except for element 3. Plaintiff alleges valid third-party contracts. (FAC, 3, 13, 24, 43, 44.) Plaintiff alleges defendant's knowledge of the agreements. (FAC, 43.) Plaintiff alleges disruption of the contracts and resulting damage. (FAC, 47, 48.) "In recent years, the tort of 'inducing breach of contract' has expanded to permit liability where the defendant does not literally induce a breach of contract, but makes plaintiff's performance of the contract 'more expensive or burdensome.'" Lipman v. Brisbane Elementary Sch. Dist. (1961) 55 Cal.2d 224, 232 (superseded by statute on other grounds). But Plaintiff does not allege sufficient facts showing intentional acts designed to induce the breach or disruption of the influencer agreements. Plaintiff merely alleges that defendant breached its obligation to pay. Plaintiff must amend to allege facts showing that defendant intended to interfere with the influencer agreements and/or lacked legitimate business reasons for its conduct. See Quelimane Co. v. Stewart Title Guaranty Co. (1998) 19 Cal.4th 26, 56. The demurrer to the fourth cause of action for intentional interference with prospective advantage is sustained without leave, pursuant to the concession in the Opposition. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: JOHN KEFFER ET AL VS. AIRBNB, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Airbnb, Inc.S Notice Of Alternative Motion And Alternative Motion To Dismiss Or Stay For Forum Non Conveniens
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 19, 2020, Line 9, 2-DEFENDANT AIRBNB, INC.'s Alternative Motion To Dismiss Or Stay For Forum Non Conveniens. Defendant Airbnb's motion to dismiss on the basis forum non conveniens is denied. "A trial court considering a forum non conveniens issue engages in a two-step process, the first of which is to determine whether a suitable alternative forum exists . . . Where there is a suitable alternative forum, the court proceeds to the next step, consideration of the private interests of the parties and the public interest in keeping the case in California." Nat'l Football League v. Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 902, 917. Here, Plaintiffs are entitled to 'due deference' of their choice of forum. Id. at 929. Further, a "corporate defendant's principal place of business is presumptively a convenient forum." Id. at 917. Here, Airbnb is a California corporation, headquartered and with its principle place of business in California. Baez Decl. ISO Opp., 2 - 4. Defendant does not show that private interest factors overcome the due deference owed to the plaintiffs' choice of venue. "The private interest factors are those that make trial and the enforceability of the ensuing judgment expeditious and relatively inexpensive, such as the ease of access to sources of proof, the cost of obtaining attendance of witnesses, and the availability of compulsory process for attendance of unwilling witnesses." Nat'l Football, 216 Cal.App.4th at 917. While the accident took place in Puerto Rico, the negligence alleged in the complaint occurred in Airbnb's corporate offices. And Defendant does not point to any specific witnesses or evidence located in Puerto Rico, only that "the critical witnesses are residents of Puerto Rico" without identifying anyone other than the host, and without evidence that the host is in fact in Puerto Rico. Def.'s MPA 10:9-10. Compare Weil & Brown, et al., California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 3:576 (Rutter: 2020) (extensive factual showing usually required for 'convenience of witnesses' venue motion). Nor does Defendant make a showing on the public interest factors. "The public interest factors include avoidance of overburdening California courts, protecting potential jurors who should not be called on to decide cases in which the local community has little concern, and weighing the competing ties of California and the alternate jurisdiction to the litigation." Nat'l Football, 216 Cal.App.4th at 917. Defendant provides no support for its argument as to overburdening. While Puerto Rico might also have an interest in this case, that does not outweigh the other factors or the deference given Plaintiffs' choice and Defendant's place of business. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
